summary.overview: Indiana is experiencing a significant energy affordability crisis, with all five major investor-owned utilities having sought or received rate increases in the past 12–18 months, drawing unprecedented public backlash and regulatory scrutiny. In February 2026 the IURC launched a formal investigative inquiry into the 'big five' utilities, followed by statewide listening sessions, while Governor Braun signed House Enrolled Act 1002 into law on February 26, 2026, introducing performance-based ratemaking, mandatory low-income assistance programs, and a new summer disconnection moratorium for low-income customers. Federal threats to eliminate LIHEAP funding compound the pressure on vulnerable Hoosier households already struggling with bills that have grown faster than incomes.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Low-income ratepayers and households in crisis
messaging_recommendations.message: You are not alone — Indiana law now requires utilities to offer low-income assistance programs and protect eligible customers from summer shutoffs; contact your utility or community action agency today to enroll before disconnections resume. Concrete, action-oriented framing (Silverman & Smith: clear call to action, audience-centered) reduces barriers to seeking help.

Indiana families are paying bills that have outpaced inflation and income growth for years; the passage of HEA 1002 is a first step, but meaningful implementation — including robust performance metrics, strong OUCC enforcement, and required low-income programs — is essential to restoring the balance between utility profits and ratepayer protection. Using data-driven, values-aligned framing (Silverman & Smith: credibility through evidence, shared values) strengthens the case for accountability.

Every Hoosier utility customer has been affected by a wave of rate increases totaling hundreds of millions of dollars; the new state affordability law and IURC investigation represent real accountability tools — but sustained public engagement is needed to ensure utilities follow through on their commitments. Narrative framing (Silverman & Smith: telling the human story, connecting personal experience to systemic change) sustains attention and public pressure.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Community action agencies and social service organizations
messaging_recommendations.message: With federal LIHEAP funding under threat and Indiana's 2025–2026 EAP application now closed, community partners should proactively connect clients to utility company assistance programs, HEA 1002 low-income enrollment protections, and IndyEAP or similar local resources before the fall application season reopens. Coalition-building framing (Silverman & Smith: mobilizing trusted messengers at the community level) ensures the most vulnerable households are reached.

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy Indiana's general base rate case — its first since 2019 — requested an approximately 16.2% increase in retail revenues (~$491.5 million annually) to be implemented in two phases. The IURC approved a reduced increase of approximately $295.7 million in annual revenues, phased in ~13% in March 2025 and ~3% in early 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: ~$295.7 million annual revenue increase (approx. $19.16/month for residential customer using 1,000 kWh; phased increase of ~13% in 2025 and ~3% in 2026)

rate_cases.description: AES Indiana filed a regulatory rate review on June 3, 2025, requesting a 13.5% base rate increase (~$216 million annually) to cover rising operational costs and infrastructure investments. A partial settlement reducing the requested increase by 53% to approximately $102.3 million was filed October 15, 2025; the OUCC and Citizens Action Coalition opposed the settlement, and the final IURC order deadline is June 24, 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Settlement proposes ~$102.3 million annual revenue increase (~$10/month for residential customer using 1,000 kWh under original request; settlement amount TBD in final order)

rate_cases.description: NIPSCO filed its 2025 electric base rate case (Cause No. 46120) requesting approximately $368.7 million in additional annual revenue (20.15% increase). A settlement agreement among most parties — including the OUCC — was approved by the IURC on June 26, 2025, approving a reduced increase phased in from July 2025 through early 2026, averaging ~$23/month (16.75%) for a residential customer using 672 kWh.
rate_cases.rate_change: ~$23/month (16.75%) for average residential customer using 672 kWh/month; phased July 2025–Q1 2026

rate_cases.description: CenterPoint Energy's 2023 electric rate case — the company's first since 2009 — was approved by the IURC on February 3, 2025 at approximately $80 million in annual revenue increases, phased in steps. The final (third) phase took effect in March 2026, resulting in a total increase of approximately $26/month for the average residential customer using 799 kWh, though CenterPoint committed to keeping base rates stable through 2027.
rate_cases.rate_change: ~$80 million annual revenue increase approved; final phase adds ~$6.86/month in March 2026, for a cumulative increase of ~$26/month for average residential customer

rate_cases.utility: IURC — Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability (all five major IOUs)
rate_cases.description: In February 2026, IURC Chair Andy Zay announced a formal Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability, compelling Indiana's five largest investor-owned utilities (AES Indiana, CenterPoint Energy Indiana, Duke Energy Indiana, Indiana Michigan Power, and NIPSCO) to appear before the commission on March 24, 2026, followed by ten statewide public listening sessions through April 22, 2026, to gather ratepayer feedback and develop short-term relief recommendations.

articles.headline: How Indy's rising electricity bills stack up
articles.summary: More than 500,000 AES Indiana customers are awaiting a final IURC ruling on a 13.5% rate hike request filed a year ago, with a decision deadline of June 24, 2026. The article benchmarks Indianapolis electricity costs against peer cities, noting that while the 17¢/kWh state average is below the national average, local bills have risen sharply due to infrastructure investments and approved tracker charges.

articles.headline: 'A real short-term crisis here': Regulators press Indiana utilities on rising energy costs
articles.summary: IURC Chair Andy Zay confronted the state's five largest utilities at a daylong affordability hearing, declaring 'we have a real short-term crisis here' and asking what immediate solutions they could offer struggling customers. AES Indiana announced it would extend its winter disconnection moratorium to May 15, 2026 and waive reconnect fees for eligible customers. The IURC then launched ten statewide listening sessions to gather direct feedback from ratepayers.

articles.headline: 'Big five' Indiana utilities defend rates in regulator questioning
articles.summary: Executives from all five major Indiana utilities testified before the IURC, arguing that customer usage patterns — not utility pricing — are the primary driver of high bills. AES Indiana President Brandi Davis-Handy said residential bills have 'largely tracked inflation over time,' while CenterPoint's Indiana president touted a commitment to stable rates through 2027. IURC commissioners pressed utilities on customer service improvements and bill transparency.

articles.headline: Indiana utility regulators call in 'big five' energy providers for affordability investigation
articles.summary: IURC Chair Andy Zay announced a formal investigative inquiry into the five largest Indiana investor-owned utilities, saying rising utility bills are 'placing added pressure on budgets that, for many, are already strained.' The inquiry, described as an 'uncharacteristic move' for the typically restrained IURC, was the first of its kind in recent memory. Governor Braun praised the move, saying it aligned with his administration's 'kitchen table' affordability focus.

articles.headline: Gov. Braun signs House Bill 1002 to tackle Indiana's energy affordability crisis
articles.summary: Governor Braun signed House Enrolled Act 1002, a major bipartisan utility reform law, on February 26, 2026. The law requires multi-year rate plans with performance incentive mechanisms tied to affordability and reliability, mandates low-income customer assistance programs, creates a summer disconnection moratorium for low-income households, and requires quarterly data reporting to the OUCC. The new levelized billing and assistance program requirements take effect July 1, 2026.

articles.headline: AES Indiana customers told regulators their stacks of utility bills don't add up
articles.summary: At an IURC listening session at Ivy Tech Community College in Indianapolis, AES Indiana customers described electric bills that had increased by as much as 58% in a single year, despite utility claims of no metering errors. State Rep. Cherrish Pryor attended and told WFYI she plans to introduce legislation next session to require IURC approval before any utility company sale. IURC Chair Zay acknowledged the financial pain residents are experiencing.

articles.headline: AES Indiana's parent company to go private in BlackRock-led $33B deal
articles.summary: AES Corporation announced a $33.4 billion deal to be taken private by a consortium led by BlackRock's Global Infrastructure Partners, CalPERS, and the Qatar Investment Authority, expected to close in late 2026 or early 2027. Indiana Democrats, including Rep. Cherrish Pryor, criticized the timing — made just after the 2026 legislative session — as an attempt to avoid regulation of utility sales. Governor Braun said his 'main criterion' for evaluating the deal is whether it will bring down rates.

articles.headline: Consumer advocates oppose proposed AES Indiana rate settlement
articles.summary: A settlement agreement in AES Indiana's rate case (Cause No. 46258) was filed with the IURC on October 15, 2025, reducing the requested base rate increase by 53% to approximately $102.3 million. The OUCC and Citizens Action Coalition declined to join the settlement and filed opposition testimony, citing insufficient justification for any increase filed less than 14 months after the utility's previous rate approval. The IURC is expected to issue a final order by June 24, 2026.

articles.headline: ICV, CAC Unveil Policy Plan to Lower Energy Costs for Hoosiers
articles.source: Citizens Action Coalition / Indiana Conservation Voters
articles.summary: Indiana Conservation Voters and Citizens Action Coalition released their 'Help Hoosiers Now: Ratepayer Relief Plan,' calling on state lawmakers to enact both immediate and long-term reforms to address rising utility costs ahead of the 2026 legislative session. The plan includes proposals for a mandatory low-income assistance program, a summer disconnection moratorium, enhanced OUCC oversight authority, and long-term rate stability mechanisms. Both organizations noted that Indiana families face some of the highest utility bill growth in the Midwest.

articles.headline: IURC to investigate energy affordability as utility costs climb
articles.summary: The IURC formally announced its Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability following months of constituent complaints about sharply rising bills, noting that all five major Indiana utilities had received or were seeking rate increases. The inquiry follows the February 2025 IURC-approved $80 million CenterPoint Energy rate hike, the January 2025 Duke Energy ~$295.7 million approval, and the June 2025 NIPSCO 16.75% electric rate approval. IURC Commissioner Pressel said the inquiry is 'a first step toward understanding and lowering utility rates.'

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chair
stakeholders.quote: "Indiana, like every state, is facing a real challenge when it comes to energy affordability. Costs are increasing across the board, and rising utility bills are placing added pressure on budgets that, for many, are already strained." (Exact quote from IURC press release)
stakeholders.source: IURC press release / Axios Indianapolis, February 25–26, 2026

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chair
stakeholders.quote: "We have a real short-term crisis here. The reality is, on Main Street, there are people that simply can't afford to pay these bills, whether they're higher or consistent." (Exact quote to AES Indiana at March 24, 2026 hearing)

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Indiana Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: "AES Indiana has not demonstrated sufficient evidence for an increase in this rate case, which it filed less than 14 months after receiving Commission approval in its last rate case. My team is recommending additional reductions that would reduce the utility's current rates and help lessen the burden on ratepayers. We received thousands of consumer comments in this case, highlighting serious hardships many customers are facing." (Exact quote from OUCC news release)
stakeholders.source: OUCC News Release regarding Cause No. 46258, September 9, 2025

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "For too long, Hoosiers have been footing the bill for decisions they didn't make and policies they didn't ask for. Indiana's regulatory system should be built to protect consumers, but that goal can only be achieved if lawmakers give regulators the tools and authority it needs. The solutions have been ready for years. The need is now incredibly urgent." (Exact quote from ICV/CAC Ratepayer Relief Plan press release)
stakeholders.source: ICV/CAC 'Help Hoosiers Now: Ratepayer Relief Plan' press release, December 2025

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "The general takeaway is customer bills are going up, they're going up a lot and they're going up soon." (Exact quote following IURC's unanimous approval of CenterPoint rate hike)

stakeholders.name: Megan Robertson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "Hoosier families are stretched thin, and they need relief now from monopoly utilities who've pushed policies for years that led to this crisis. This plan gives state leaders a clear, practical path to lower bills that will protect Hoosiers most in need and build a more affordable, reliable energy future for every community in our state." (Exact quote from Ratepayer Relief Plan press release)
stakeholders.source: ICV/CAC 'Help Hoosiers Now: Ratepayer Relief Plan' press release, December 2025

stakeholders.name: Brandi Davis-Handy
stakeholders.title: President
stakeholders.quote: "We have a deep commitment to operating efficiently and keeping rates as low as possible. Throughout this process, we've listened to stakeholder feedback and evaluated tradeoffs we will make for future investments." (Exact quote at time of AES rate case settlement announcement)

stakeholders.name: Cherrish Pryor
stakeholders.title: State Representative
stakeholders.quote: "People are feeling like they are prisoners of the utility companies. And people have reached a point where they are not just mad, they're pissed off, and if legislators don't wake up and see [it] as a problem, people are going to [react]." (Exact quote to WFYI at IURC listening session)

stakeholders.name: Cherrish Pryor
stakeholders.title: State Representative
stakeholders.quote: "AES has continued to put shareholders' profits above the needs of working families. Selling to private equity will only make it worse." (Exact quote from news release responding to AES/BlackRock deal)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle / IPB News, March 2, 2026

stakeholders.name: Jim Pressel
stakeholders.title: State Representative
stakeholders.quote: "Hoosiers in our community are struggling with high utility bills and we want to see solutions. We are now asking the IURC and NIPSCO to review current utility rates and take more consideration of the customers." (Exact quote from news release)

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor
stakeholders.quote: "From the beginning, my administration has been focused on kitchen table issues like affordability. That is why I appointed a strong consumer advocate in the Office of the Utility Consumer Counselor and appointed IURC commissioners focused on ratepayers. I expect Chairman Zay to keep the focus on addressing affordability, transparency, and reliability for ratepayers while holding investor-owned utilities accountable." (Exact quote from statement on IURC affordability investigation)
